content.text: A maintainer ownership model defines the roles, responsibilities, and accountability structures for the engineers who own inner-source repositories. A maintainer is a named individual (or a small named team) who has accepted formal accountability for a shared repository's quality, direction, and long-term health. This accountability includes triaging issues, guiding the project roadmap, making final decisions on contested contributions, managing releases, coordinating security disclosures, and planning for their own succession. The maintainer model transforms shared code from an orphaned artifact to a cared-for project with a human face.

Maintainership is distinct from authorship (who wrote the code originally) and from trusted committer status (who has merge authority). An author may have moved to another team; a trusted committer may have merge authority but no obligation to triage issues or plan releases. The maintainer role is specifically the role that cannot be left vacant without the project deteriorating: if no one is triaging issues, users give up filing them; if no one is planning releases, contributions pile up unshipped; if no one is doing succession planning, a maintainer departure leaves the project in limbo.

Shared libraries without clear maintainers accumulate a predictable failure pattern: initial enthusiasm, growing issue backlog, declining contributor confidence, project abandonment, and eventual replacement by competing per-team implementations. The pattern repeats because the root cause is never addressed: no named individual feels personally accountable for the library's health. Naming a maintainer with explicit responsibilities interrupts this cycle at the source.

The maintainer model also provides consuming teams with a known contact point. When a library has a bug that blocks a team's deployment, knowing exactly who to contact — and knowing that person has an SLA for responding to bugs — is the difference between a resolved incident and a multi-day unblock. Without a named maintainer, consuming teams may file an issue that sits unseen, escalate to their own management, and ultimately patch their local copy of the library, adding to the divergence problem inner-source is meant to solve.
Named accountability eliminates the bystander effect where multiple teams each assume someone else is triaging issues.
Consuming teams have a known escalation path when a library issue blocks their work.
Release cadence and versioning discipline improve when a specific person owns the release process.
Security disclosures can be coordinated with a known contact rather than searching for the right person under time pressure.
Succession planning prevents project abandonment when a maintainer changes roles or leaves the organization.
Maintainers accumulate deep knowledge of the library's design and usage patterns, enabling better architectural decisions.
Maintainer role is an additional responsibility on top of a primary team role; organizations that do not protect time for maintainer work will see the role become nominal.
A single-person maintainer team is a key-person risk; the role should have a co-maintainer or understudy from the beginning.
Maintainers may develop blind spots about their library's limitations, preferring improvements they understand over contributions that challenge their design choices.
Rotating maintainership distributes the burden but reduces the depth of accumulated knowledge in any one person.
Maintainer authority can create gatekeeping friction if the maintainer has a narrow view of the library's appropriate scope.

The platform team establishes a maintainer registry that maps each inner-source repository to its current maintainer(s) and trusted committers. The registry is published in the internal developer portal and linked from each repository's README. When an inner-source repository is first designated, the platform team works with the contributing team to identify a willing, appropriately experienced maintainer and documents the appointment with the maintainer's manager's acknowledgment that time will be protected for the role.

Maintainers operate against a defined set of SLAs and responsibilities published in the program documentation: issue acknowledgment within 24 hours, bug triage within 5 business days, security vulnerability response within 48 hours, and a minimum release cadence (monthly for active libraries, quarterly for stable ones). The platform team measures SLA compliance through dashboards that track issue age, PR queue depth, and release frequency, and follows up with maintainers or their managers when metrics deteriorate.

Succession planning is built into the maintainer lifecycle. Maintainers are expected to identify and mentor a co-maintainer who can take over the role. When a maintainer anticipates a role change or departure, they initiate a formal transition: the incoming maintainer shadows the current maintainer for at least one release cycle, the institutional knowledge is documented in the repository wiki, and the maintainer registry is updated. Projects that undergo an unplanned maintainer departure (unexpected resignation, sudden role change) are flagged by the platform team for emergency succession.

Every inner-source repository must have a named maintainer before it is opened for external contributions. A repository with no assigned maintainer should not be listed as inner-source; it should be designated as archived or as a single-team repository until a maintainer is identified. The platform team's inner-source registry should enforce this: the status of a repository transitions to inner-source only after a maintainer is documented and has accepted the role's responsibilities.

Reassess maintainer assignments annually or whenever a repository's contribution volume changes significantly. A library that initially had light contribution traffic may grow to require a co-maintainer to maintain review SLAs. A library that was once active may settle into a maintenance mode where the maintainer role requires only quarterly effort. The level of investment in the maintainer role should match the library's current activity, not its peak activity or initial intent.

# MAINTAINERS.md — repository ownership record

## Current maintainers

| Role | Name | Team | GitHub | Since |
|------|------|------|--------|-------|
| Primary Maintainer | Alice Chen | Platform Engineering | @achen | 2024-03 |
| Co-Maintainer | Bob Kim | Data Tools | @bkim | 2024-09 |

## Trusted Committers

| Name | Team | GitHub | Since |
|------|------|--------|-------|
| Carol Davis | ML Infra | @cdavis | 2024-06 |
| David Lee | Agents Team | @dlee | 2024-11 |

## Maintainer responsibilities

- Acknowledge all new issues within 24 hours (business days)
- Triage and label bugs within 5 business days
- Review or assign PRs within 5 business days
- Publish a release at least monthly when there are pending changes
- Respond to security disclosures within 48 hours
- Maintain the CHANGELOG.md and MIGRATION.md for every major version

## Succession plan

Co-maintainer Bob Kim will take over as primary maintainer if Alice steps down.
Succession timeline: minimum 4 weeks of overlap + one release cycle.

## Contact

For urgent issues: #inner-source-agent-tools Slack channel
For security disclosures: security@company.internal (private)

# scripts/maintainer_health.py — metrics script run by platform team weekly
"""
Checks maintainer health metrics for inner-source repositories.
Reports: open issue age, open PR age, days since last release, SLA violations.
"""
import json
from datetime import datetime, timezone, timedelta
from github import Github  # PyGitHub

INNER_SOURCE_REPOS = [
    "company/agent-tool-library",
    "company/prompt-templates",
    "company/eval-fixtures",
]

ISSUE_ACK_SLA_DAYS = 1
BUG_TRIAGE_SLA_DAYS = 5
MAX_PR_AGE_DAYS = 5
MIN_RELEASE_CADENCE_DAYS = 45

def check_repo_health(repo_name: str, g: Github) -> dict:
    repo = g.get_repo(repo_name)
    now = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
    issues = list(repo.get_issues(state="open"))
    prs = list(repo.get_pulls(state="open"))
    releases = list(repo.get_releases())

    stale_issues = [i for i in issues if (now - i.created_at).days > BUG_TRIAGE_SLA_DAYS]
    stale_prs = [p for p in prs if (now - p.created_at).days > MAX_PR_AGE_DAYS]
    last_release_age = (now - releases[0].created_at).days if releases else 999

    return {
        "repo": repo_name,
        "open_issues": len(issues),
        "stale_issues": len(stale_issues),
        "stale_prs": len(stale_prs),
        "days_since_release": last_release_age,
        "sla_violations": (
            len(stale_issues) > 0 or
            len(stale_prs) > 0 or
            last_release_age > MIN_RELEASE_CADENCE_DAYS
        ),
    }

if __name__ == "__main__":
    g = Github()  # uses GITHUB_TOKEN env var
    results = [check_repo_health(r, g) for r in INNER_SOURCE_REPOS]
    print(json.dumps(results, indent=2))
    violations = [r for r in results if r["sla_violations"]]
    if violations:
        print(f"\nSLA violations in {len(violations)} repos:")
        for v in violations:
            print(f"  {v['repo']}: stale_issues={v['stale_issues']}, "
                  f"stale_prs={v['stale_prs']}, days_since_release={v['days_since_release']}")

Alice is appointed as the primary maintainer for the agent-tool-library. Her manager acknowledges the appointment and agrees to protect 20% of her time for maintainer work (approximately one day per week). Alice's first action is to update the MAINTAINERS.md file, establish issue labels (bug, feature, security, needs-triage), and set up the weekly metrics script to alert her in Slack when SLAs are approaching violation. She also identifies Bob from the data-tools team as a co-maintainer candidate and invites him to shadow her for the next two releases.

Six months into Alice's tenure, a security vulnerability is reported via security@company.internal in the HTTP client wrapper tool. Alice receives the report, acknowledges it within 4 hours, assesses the severity (CVSS 7.5, moderate), and coordinates with the platform security team on a disclosure plan. Within 36 hours, she patches the vulnerability, releases v2.4.2, and sends a notification to consuming teams via the inner-source channel with instructions for upgrading. The total time from disclosure to patch availability was within the 48-hour SLA.

After two years in the role, Alice is promoted to a staff engineering position that leaves no time for library maintainer work. She initiates a formal transition: she documents three months of architectural context in the repository wiki, mentors Bob through the next major release cycle, and transfers the maintainer registration to Bob. The transition takes four weeks and is completed before her last day in the previous role. The platform team updates the registry and sends a transition announcement to the inner-source channel so that consuming teams know their contact has changed.

The Python Software Foundation's model for CPython maintainership is a well-documented example of this stewardship model at large scale. CPython has a Steering Council that handles governance, core developers who serve as trusted committers, and subsystem maintainers responsible for specific parts of the standard library. Responsibilities are explicit: maintainers are expected to triage bugs, review PRs, and respond to security disclosures. The Dev Guide (devguide.python.org) documents exactly what is expected of each role, how to become a core developer, and how to handle disputes. The InnerSource Commons patterns collection (innersourcecommons.org/learn/patterns) provides inner-source-specific adaptations of this governance model for organizational contexts.
Naming a maintainer without protecting time for the role: a nominal maintainer who cannot fulfill the SLAs is worse than no maintainer because it creates false confidence.
Allowing the maintainer role to be concentrated in one person with no succession plan, creating project-ending key-person risk.
Not measuring SLA compliance; maintainers who are consistently violating SLAs need support (more time, a co-maintainer), not more pressure.
Rotating maintainerships on a fixed schedule regardless of whether successors are ready, producing knowledge gaps at every transition.
Treating maintainership as a purely technical role; it requires interpersonal skills for managing contributor relationships and resolving contribution disputes.
Not separating the maintainer role from the trusted committer role: merging both into one person who then becomes the bottleneck for all PRs.
Protect explicit time for maintainer work in the maintainer's sprint planning; inner-source cannot compete with primary team deliverables without protected time.
Require a co-maintainer (or documented understudy) for every inner-source repository with significant contributor activity.
Publish a public SLA document and enforce it through automated monitoring that notifies maintainers and their managers of approaching violations.
Conduct maintainer retrospectives quarterly to identify what is working and what is creating friction in the maintainer workflow.
Invest in tooling (issue templates, PR templates, CI automation) that reduces the per-issue and per-PR overhead for maintainers.
Recognize maintainers formally in engineering review processes; maintainer contributions to organizational shared infrastructure should count as evidence of senior engineering impact.

The maintainer model scales with the number of repositories and the volume of contributions, not with the number of consuming teams. An inner-source repository with 5 active contributors submitting 2 PRs per week requires approximately 3-4 hours of maintainer review time per week. A repository with 20 contributors submitting 10 PRs per week requires a co-maintainer and distributed trusted committers to maintain SLAs without overwhelming the primary maintainer. Measure contribution volume and adjust maintainer capacity accordingly.

Cross-repository concerns — a change in the platform's base library that affects all inner-source repositories, a security policy update that changes contribution requirements — require coordination across maintainers. The platform team should convene a monthly or quarterly maintainers forum where these cross-cutting issues can be discussed and resolved. Without this forum, cross-repository changes are implemented inconsistently, and maintainers learn about them through informal channels rather than through structured communication.

The maintainer is the security contact for the repository: the person who receives vulnerability disclosures, assesses severity, coordinates patches, and manages public communication. This role requires that the maintainer's contact information is current in the security disclosure channel, that they have the authority to cut an emergency release without waiting for the normal release cadence, and that they know the platform security team's escalation path for vulnerabilities that require coordinated disclosure with external parties.

Maintainer knowledge concentration is a reliability risk: if the maintainer is the only person who understands the release process, a maintainer absence during a critical security incident can delay patch availability. Document the release process step-by-step in the repository wiki, ensure that the co-maintainer has performed at least one complete release cycle, and test the emergency release path periodically in a non-production context so that the process is well-understood before it is needed under pressure.
The maintainer role is distinct from authorship and trusted committer status: it is explicit accountability for a repository's long-term health.
Named maintainability with published SLAs eliminates the bystander effect that causes unowned shared libraries to deteriorate.
Time protection from the maintainer's manager is a prerequisite for effective maintainership; a nominal maintainer with no time is worse than no maintainer.
Succession planning must be initiated before the maintainer departs; knowledge transfer takes weeks, not days.
Automated SLA monitoring with escalation to managers creates the organizational accountability needed to sustain the role.
Maintainer contributions should be formally recognized in engineering review processes as evidence of organizational impact.
